questions.optionB: Inhibiting cell wall synthesis (blocking transpeptidase/PBP → prevents peptidoglycan cross-linking)
questions.explanation: Penicillins: β-lactam antibiotics → bind to PBPs (penicillin-binding proteins/transpeptidases) → inhibit cross-linking of peptidoglycan cell wall → weakened wall → osmotic lysis (bactericidal). Resistance: β-lactamase production (overcome by adding clavulanic acid, sulbactam, tazobactam). Types: natural penicillin (Pen G/V), aminopenicillins (ampicillin, amoxicillin), anti-staphylococcal (methicillin, nafcillin), antipseudomonal (piperacillin). Reference: KD Tripathi — Penicillins.

questions.questionText: Chloramphenicol causes which serious adverse effect?
questions.optionB: Aplastic anemia (idiosyncratic, dose-independent, irreversible) and gray baby syndrome (neonates)
questions.explanation: Chloramphenicol: bacteriostatic, inhibits 50S ribosomal peptidyl transferase (blocks protein synthesis). Serious adverse effects: (1) Aplastic anemia (rare but fatal — idiosyncratic, NOT dose-related), (2) Dose-dependent reversible bone marrow suppression, (3) Gray baby syndrome in neonates (immature liver cannot conjugate → accumulation → cardiovascular collapse, ashen gray color). Broad spectrum. Used for: typhoid (historically), meningitis, rickettsial infections. Reference: KD Tripathi — Chloramphenicol.

questions.optionB: Nephrotoxicity (reversible) and ototoxicity (irreversible — vestibular and cochlear damage)
questions.explanation: Aminoglycosides: bactericidal, bind 30S ribosome → cause misreading of mRNA and inhibit translocation. Concentration-dependent killing. Toxicities: (1) Nephrotoxicity (proximal tubule damage — usually reversible), (2) Ototoxicity (irreversible damage to vestibular and cochlear hair cells — tinnitus, vertigo, hearing loss), (3) Neuromuscular blockade (rare — exacerbated by MG). Monitor trough levels. Synergistic with β-lactams for endocarditis (enterococcal). Reference: KD Tripathi — Aminoglycosides.

questions.optionB: Inhibition of bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (blocks transcription)
questions.explanation: Rifampin (rifampicin): inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (β-subunit of RNA polymerase) → blocks mRNA synthesis. Bactericidal. Key drug in TB treatment (kills semi-dormant bacilli in macrophages). Also: leprosy, meningococcal/H. influenzae prophylaxis, brucellosis, staphylococcal prosthetic joint infections. Potent CYP450 inducer (red/orange discoloration of body fluids). Drug interactions: decreases efficacy of OCP, warfarin, protease inhibitors, cyclosporine. Reference: KD Tripathi — Rifampin.

questions.optionB: Inhibiting DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV → blocking DNA replication
questions.explanation: Fluoroquinolones: bactericidal, inhibit DNA gyrase (Gram-negatives, primarily) and topoisomerase IV (Gram-positives). Broad spectrum. Ciprofloxacin: Pseudomonas, GNR, anthrax. Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin: respiratory pathogens ('respiratory fluoroquinolones'). Adverse effects: tendon rupture (Achilles), QT prolongation, CNS effects, photosensitivity, cartilage damage in children (avoid <18 years, pregnant/nursing). Contraindicated in children and pregnant women (traditionally). Reference: KD Tripathi — Fluoroquinolones.

questions.questionText: The DOTS strategy for tuberculosis uses which drug regimen?
questions.optionB: Intensive phase: HRZE (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) × 2 months, then HR × 4 months
questions.explanation: DOTS (Directly Observed Therapy, Short-course): standard TB treatment. Category I (new cases): Intensive phase — 2 months HRZE (Isoniazid, Rifampin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) → Continuation phase — 4 months HR. Total: 6 months. INH side effects: hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine/B6), SLE-like syndrome. Ethambutol: optic neuritis (red-green color blindness). Pyrazinamide: hyperuricemia, hepatotoxicity. Multi-drug therapy prevents resistance. Reference: KD Tripathi — Anti-TB Drugs.
